title: Technical Overview
text:
[ADD ME: DIAGRAM]

Zarr is a chunked, compressed array storage specification and format. Zarr is designed for cloud-native (network-addressable chunks in object storage) access. Zarr is the proposed specification for structuring virtual stores at NASA.
Chunk manifests are lightweight metadata structures that describe a mapping from logical data space to where that data is stored. Another term for chunk manifests is an “indirection layer”. Icechunk and Kerchunk are 2 common implementations of chunk manifests.
Icechunk is a transactional storage engine for Zarr arrays. Icechunk stores chunk manifests which it calls virtual datasets.
Kerchunk is an early approach to creating chunk manifests (what it calls reference files) which maps virtual Zarr array coordinates to byte ranges in existing files using the JSON or Parquet file formats for persistence.

title: Technical Overview
text:
[ADD ME: DIAGRAM]

Chunk manifests are lightweight metadata structures that describe a mapping from logical data space to where that data is stored. Another term for chunk manifests is an “indirection layer”. Icechunk and Kerchunk are 2 common implementations of chunk manifests.
Icechunk is a transactional storage engine for Zarr arrays. Icechunk stores chunk manifests which it calls virtual datasets.
Kerchunk is an early approach to creating chunk manifests (what it calls reference files) which maps virtual Zarr array coordinates to byte ranges in existing files using the JSON or Parquet file formats for persistence.

title: Technical Overview
text:
GeoZarr and multiscales
GeoZarr extends Zarr with geospatial conventions. including coordinate reference systems and spatial metadata. Multiscale representations allow efficient access at varying levels of aggregation.
Adoption of GeoZarr is recommended for future virtual store implementations to ensure interoperability and long-term maintainability. Adoption of multiscales should be considered, especially for high resolution data.

title: Introduction
text: This report assesses the current state and future direction of virtual store technology for NASA data. It documents mature implementations, developing approaches, known limitations, governance gaps, and recommended next steps.

title: Introduction
text: This report assesses the current state and future direction of virtual store technology for NASA data. It documents mature implementations, developing approaches, known limitations, governance gaps, and recommended next steps.

title: Introduction
section: Summary of recommendations and current state
text:
Summary of recommendations and current state
Virtual store technology is ready for production use with consistently gridded data and is actively being developed for more complex data types. Key recommendations include adopting Icechunk, GeoZarr and multiscales standards, engaging with any evolving chunk manifest protocol, planning for EGIS integration, and addressing governance gaps.
See the Recommendations section for the full summary.

title: Virtual Stores at NASA
text: ⚠️ UNDER DEVELOPMENT ⚠️

title: Virtual Stores at NASA
section: What are virtual stores and what do they enable?
text:
What are virtual stores and what do they enable?
Core to virtual store technology is lightweight metadata pointing to data byte ranges in existing files. Virtual store technology enables data users to access subsets of large scientific datasets without downloading, scanning and pre-processing any files.

title: Virtual Stores at NASA
section: Vision: NASA datasets accessible through a single entrypoint
text:
Vision: NASA datasets accessible through a single entrypoint
The long-term vision is that many NASA datasets are discoverable and accessible through a single entrypoint (per dataset). Users will not have to be aware of the underlying data format or storage location.

title: Virtual Stores at NASA
text:
Benefits for data users

Logical dataset access access large number of files, without downloading any data
Consistent access patterns across diverse data types

title: Virtual Stores at NASA
section: Benefits for data providers and NASA
text:
Benefits for data providers and NASA

Cost savings through reduced egress and compute
Providing analysis-ready access to existing archives without reformatting

title: Virtualization Examples
text: This page collects worked examples of virtual store implementations, serving as a reference for virtual layer producers. Each example documents the data type, chosen approach, design decisions, and lessons learned.

title: Virtualization Examples
text:
How to use this library
Find the example most similar to your dataset and use case. Adapt the patterns to your specific data, keeping in mind the trade-offs described in Known Limitations and Best Practices.

title: Governance Decisions Required
section: Versioning and update policies for virtual layers
text:
Versioning and update policies for virtual layers
Virtual stores are living artifacts that will need to be updated as source data is corrected, reprocessed, or extended. Policies are needed to govern:

When and how virtual store versions are incremented
How consumers are notified of updates
How to handle backward compatibility

(Versioning policies to be defined.)

title: Applications of Virtual Store Technology at NASA
text:
Collection-level aggregated chunk manifests have been successfully implemented at PODAAC for consistently gridded datasets. This represents the most mature pattern and is recommended as the baseline approach for similar data types across DAACs.
[ADD ME: PODAAC Virtual Stores Documentation @DeanHenze @edshred2000]
Characteristics:

Consistent spatial and temporal resolution across each variable
Uniform chunk grid and compression scheme across each variable
Chunk manifest generated at collection level, spanning many granules

title: Applications of Virtual Store Technology at NASA
text:
Developing implementations

Displacement data (NISAR)
Synthetic aperture radar displacement products from ASF are an active area of development for virtual store approaches.
[ADD ME: NISAR Virtual Store Documentation @jhkennedy]


L2 Collections at PODAAC
[ADD ME: PODAAC L2 Virtual Stores Documentation @DeanHenze @edshred2000]


Architectures for Ongoing Data
Appending new data to an existing virtual store requires different strategies depending on the underlying technology:

Icechunk: supports transactional appends natively
Kerchunk: typically requires overwriting the reference JSON



Non-uniform data
Some NASA collections present additional complexity:

Varying compression schemes and chunk grids across a logical array (usually across granules). We often call this “quirky” data.
L2 (often orbital swath data) with varying coordinates and projections.

These patterns are under active investigation.
[ADD ME: More about those developments from @maxrjones and @d-v-b]

title: Recommendations Summary
text:
The most important design input for a virtual store is a clear understanding of how the data will actually be accessed. Chunk layout and manifest structure should be optimized for the dominant access pattern of the intended user community.


For NISAR data where time series analysis is the primary use case, a frame-oriented chunk manifest design ensures efficient per-frame loading. This decision should be made deliberately, with awareness of the trade-offs for other access patterns (see Known Limitations).

title: Recommendations Summary
text:
The most important design input for a virtual store is a clear understanding of how the data will actually be accessed. Chunk layout and manifest structure should be optimized for the dominant access pattern of the intended user community.


For NISAR data where time series analysis is the primary use case, a frame-oriented chunk manifest design ensures efficient per-frame loading. This decision should be made deliberately, with awareness of the trade-offs for other access patterns (see Known Limitations).

title: Recommendations Summary
text:
Adopt GeoZarr standards
GeoZarr should be adopted for all new virtual store implementations. This ensures geospatial interoperability and positions NASA data for long-term maintainability as the ecosystem matures.

title: Recommendations Summary
section: Engage with the evolving chunk manifest protocol
text:
Engage with the evolving chunk manifest protocol
TBD: The chunk manifest protocol specification is speculative at this time.

title: Recommendations Summary
text:
Prioritize EGIS integration planning
Integration with the Earthdata Geographic Information System (EGIS) has been identified as a future priority. Scoping should begin to ensure integration with EGIS is smooth.

title: Recommendations Summary
section: Address governance gaps identified in Section 6
text:
Address governance gaps identified in Section 6
The governance decisions identified in Governance — metadata placement standards, versioning policies, and stewardship ownership — should be resolved before virtual store technology is deployed more broadly across DAACs.

title: Known Limitations
text: Icechunk is written in Rust with an API in Python. Users and data providers working in other languages (Julia, R, Java, etc.) may face limited or no support for reading and writing Icechunk stores.

title: Known Limitations
text: Icechunk is written in Rust with an API in Python. Users and data providers working in other languages (Julia, R, Java, etc.) may face limited or no support for reading and writing Icechunk stores.

title: Known Limitations
section: Early structural decisions have lasting performance consequences
text:
Early structural decisions have lasting performance consequences
File chunking and chunk manifests cannot simultaneously optimize for all use cases. Further, chunk manifests depend on the chunking already inherent to the files. You cannot create a chunk manifest to access a unit smaller than chunks in the underlying files. The implication is, for example, if a set of files is optimized for spatial access it cannot simultaneously be optimized for access across the time dimension (i.e. time series).
[ADD ME: DIAGRAM OF PANCAKES AND CHURROS]

title: Virtual Stores and the NASA Data System
text:
There are a number of components to consider when envisioning virtual stores and their place in the broad NASA data ecosystem.
Virtual stores provide a uniform interface to archival data through the Zarr data model and specification. However, to access and visualize this data, the ecosystem needs to adapt to integrate the Zarr data model and specification. While xarray and zarr-python provide powerful tools for working with this data, a number of existing powerful tools at NASA need to be considered to support NASA users.
